Name: 2_agent_identity_chain_layer
Representative_Docs: ["this feature request nails the three missing primitives for production agent deployments. a few thoughts from building agent identity infrastructure: on cryptographic identity: the per-agent ed25519 keypair approach is sound, but the harder problem is cross-organizational verification. when agent a (built with langchain in org x) calls a tool in org y, org y needs to verify agent a's identity without access to org x's key infrastructure. this is where on-chain attestation helps identity proofs stored on a public ledger that any party can verify without bilateral trust. satp (solana agent trust protocol) uses this pattern: 45 attestation types across 14 proof categories, stored as immutable memos. any mcp server can verify an agent's trust score without knowing the agent's organization. on intent verification: the schema is the right abstraction. worth noting that ietf draft-klrc (transaction tokens for agents, march 2026) proposes a similar model at the protocol level scoped tokens that encode intent, authorized-by, constraints, and expiration. the ai trust commons recently cited three incidents (spotify honk, amazon kiro, meta rogue agent) where agents had valid identity and authorization but no intent boundaries. intent verification is becoming the next standard layer. on kill switches: kill switches need identity to work. you can't revoke what you can't identify. the pattern that scales: identity revocation list real-time propagation. hol (hashgraph online) achieves sub-3s global revocation propagation through consensus service messages. satp achieves it through attestation invalidation on-chain. practical integration path for langchain: rather than building identity from scratch in langchain-core, consider making it pluggable a interface that external identity systems can implement: this lets teams choose their trust backend (satp, agentid, hol, enterprise iam) without coupling langchain to any single identity system. the npm package already implements this pattern for mcp-based verification. would be interested in contributing a reference implementation if the langchain team is open to prs on this.", "thanks, ( this is a useful extension of the discussion. the cross-org case is where problem becomes much harder. once an agent is calling tools outside its own deployment, static credentials alone are a weak basis for trust. we would still separate the layers very clearly: cryptographic identity helps establish who is calling attestations reputation can improve trust calibration across organizations execution-time enforcement determines whether the action can proceed at all so we see behavioral reputation as a valuable input, especially for cross-org authorization, but not as a substitute for a fail-closed execution boundary. in compact form: if that last step is not binding, the rest remains advisory. so the direction you describe is complementary, not competing: identity strengthens provenance, reputation strengthens trust decisions, and the boundary is what makes those decisions operational. great discussion. i want to address the three-layer model directly, because aip already covers all three and crucially, addresses 's point about execution-time binding. layer 1: cryptographic identity ed25519 keypair per agent, did-based addressing ( ). this is shipping today . layer 2: intent verification kill switch 8-step verification pipeline: version check schema validation expiry nonce replay signature (ed25519 or hmac) boundary enforcement (action allowlists, monetary limits, geo restrictions) revocation check attestation trust score. kill switch revokes globally with zero propagation delay via the revocation mesh. layer 3: behavioral reputation aip includes a bayesian trust score engine. trust is earned, not assigned computed from: - completion rate (successful total verifications) - violation history (boundary violations penalized) - revocation history (past revocations reduce trust) - attestation stability (frequent model changes penalized) - delegation depth (deeper chains less trusted) - age factor (longer history more trusted) a new agent starts at trust score 0.0. an agent with 500 successful verifications and zero violations earns a high score. this is exactly the behavioral signal describes without requiring blockchain infrastructure. for cross-org: the hosted platform ( korven.cc ( provides persistent trust scores that survive restarts and are shared across deployments. the trust history follows the agent's did, not the deployment. on execution-time enforcement ( ) this is the critical point and aip's module implements exactly the fail-closed pattern you describe. the enforcement chain: this is not advisory. the wrapper creates the envelope, signs it, verifies it through the pipeline, and only calls the wrapped function if is true. the tool call physically cannot proceed it's the same pattern as a tls handshake failure preventing data transmission. this works at three levels: - class decorator, all public methods gated - wrap any individual function - patch a running instance (works with crewai, langchain agents, any python object) on the interface happy to implement this. aip already exposes the right primitives: the key difference: aip gives you all three layers identity, intent verification, and behavioral trust in a single with zero external infrastructure dependencies. teams that need cross-org persistence can connect to aip cloud. teams that don't can run fully local. open to co-authoring a pr. our sdks: - python: ( pypi ( - typescript: 31 31 conformance tests passing ( github.com theaniketgiri aip ( - spec: rfc-001 (", "this thread converged on three properties that a production agent identity system needs: 1. enforcement fail-closed gate that blocks unauthorized execution ( , ) 2. admissibility structural proof that the transition was valid, not just that it was blocked ( , ) 3. behavioral evidence signed record of what actually happened, for forensics and kill-switch traceability ( ) the agent passport system ( implements all three as a single protocol stack. 103 modules, 2,189 tests. npm and pypi: how it maps to what this thread asked for: cryptographic identity ed25519 keypair per agent, signed passport with delegation chains. the identity is framework-agnostic ( based), so the same passport works across langchain, crewai, autogen, or any mcp server: delegation with monotonic narrowing 's point about cross-domain authority is handled by scoped delegation chains. each delegation can only narrow scope, never widen it. a sub-agent can never have more authority than its delegator: enforcement admissibility (3-signature chain) this is the core of 's distinction. aps doesn't just block it produces a cryptographic proof chain for every action: 1. agent declares what it wants to do (signed by agent) 2. evaluator checks against floor delegation (signed by evaluator) 3. executor proves what was done (signed by executor) three signatures. the intent is admissible (structurally valid transition), the decision is enforceable (fail-closed gate), and the receipt is evidence (behavioral record). 's point about cosigned records this is exactly that, with the evaluator as an independent third party. kill switch (cascade revocation): behavioral evidence chain each carries an optional , linking receipts into an append-only chain. after a kill switch fires, you trace back through the chain to find exactly which action in which agent caused the problem. this is what identified as the missing piece. on 's point about decoupling identity from frameworks agreed. aps identity is framework-agnostic by design. the passport and delegation chain work the same whether the agent runs on langchain, crewai, bare mcp, or a custom orchestrator. the enforcement layer sits between the agent and whatever tools it calls it doesn't require framework integration, just a gateway check before execution. the mcp server ( ) exposes 125 tools that any mcp-compatible client can call directly, including , , for the full 3-sig chain. happy to help scope a langchain integration pattern if there's interest. the cleanest path is a wrapper that calls before fail-closed, with a signed receipt chain as output."]

'field': 'city', 'orderby': 'field': 'totalrevenue', 'direction': 'desc' , 'orderby':", "ok, so now you've slid from dynamic responseformat into dynamic tools and are treating those as the same thing. you also assuming is somehow proof that should be dynamically swappable, which it isn t. let me first spell out what you re missing --- 1. tools (providerstrategy toolstrategy autostrategy) is about the shape of the model s final response for that node. tools ( ) are actions the agent can call (via and ). saying thing like: dynamic toolstrategy should work like dynamic tools: pass a placeholder at construction, rebuild it in . but: dynamic tools are a problem. dynamic is a structured output problem. those are different plumbing paths. --- 2. doesn t use at all you're reference example: builds a dynamic schema with . calls on a side model (the selector). parses the result and then mutates . that is not using , and it is not using . it s just: side llm call with structured output choose tools update call main agent normally. so it s not evidence that should be dynamic; it s an entirely separate pattern. --- 3. why your dynamic toolstrategy mental model is off you are imagining: declare a placeholder structured output tool at , then rebuild its definition per turn in . but inside the agent: turns the schema into synthetic structured output tools once, at time. those synthetic tools live in , not in . the graph wiring and routing logic depend on that set being stable. enforces: any used later must be a subset of that fixed set. so your proposed code that dynamically rebuilds a tool in is talking about real tools ( ), while the original issue is about structured output ( toolstrategy providerstrategy). your re mixing the two layers. --- in depth summary: the middleware sketch you posted is a good pattern for dynamic tools, but it s solving a different problem than this issue, which was about dynamic structured output. there are really three distinct pieces here: 1. real tools these are the things in that run through and can be wrapped via . what you describe (register a base tool in , then replace or augment it per call in or ) is totally reasonable for that class of tools. 2. provider native structured output ( ) this is the json schema the model natively understands path. here, , and we can safely change the schema per call in middleware because there are no synthetic tools; we just send a different schema to the model and parse the result back. 3. tool based structured output ( ) this is the fallback when the provider doesn t support native json schema. in this mode we turn the schema into synthetic structured output tools once at , store them in , and wire the agent graph (model tools loop, exit conditions, error handling) around that fixed set. at runtime, explicitly requires that any you hand it only refers to that pre declared set; middleware is allowed to narrow (subset), but not introduce a brand new structured tool. so your proposed dynamic tool middleware is perfectly aligned with (1): it plays with and real tools, which is exactly what does after its side structured output call. the original feature request that started this thread here, though, is about (2) and (3): - for dynamic schemas, the intended path is (2) provider native structured output where we can freely do per call. - in toolstrategy mode, the set of structured output tools is intentionally treated as a compile time contract today; making it dynamic would mean relaxing the invariants around and rethinking how the graph and error handling are wired. re do you envision a dynamic toolstrategy : conceptually, yes, it s possible, but it s a non trivial change (it s not just swap a tool in ). yes, langchain developers would need to design a version of toolstrategy that allows a bounded family of schemas tools to be declared up front and then safely specialized at runtime without breaking routing or validation. that s a larger design discussion than this issue. for your concrete use case (state dependent ): - if you can use a provider with native structured output, in middleware is the supported pattern. - if your main workhorse is a toolstrategy only model like with tools, you can either: do a side classification hop on a structured output capable model and write the result into state, or temporarily override for that one call, then switch back in later middleware. toolstrategy is conservative for now and avoides making the structured output tool set mutable at runtime, but the dynamic toolstrategy idea is something they can potentially consider separately as a significantly more advanced feature if they'd be inclined to do so. p.s. i m not a langchain maintainer, haha. just someone who s really interested in low level llm application building and constantly iterating on my own understanding and code by deep diving well designed codebases (like langchain, especially v1.0). if i ve misrepresented anything in the implementation or explanations, i m definitely happy to be corrected."]

Representative_Docs: ["hey joaquín, meant to get back to you earlier last week, got swamped with work. my apologies. attached is the integration code (langchainmintcallback.py). what i did with flowing: - forked the repo - added the mint callback handler (wraps langchain's lifecycle hooks) - tasks came in via runtask, work completed,recordjobfired on-chain when i tested it a few days ago 4 tasks came through, mint settled, tokens verified in wallet. your task structure integrated easily with the job recording format without needing to force anything. the callback is pretty simple as it tracks start end time, generates a job hash, submits to solana on completion. nothing fancy, just wiring the lifecycle to settlement. please keep me updated on flowing's progress, very interested to see where this goes. cheers on wednesday, february 4th, 2026 at 3:04 pm, joaquin arias . wrote: joaquinariasco-lab left a comment (langchain-ai langchain 34979) ( hey, this is really great to hear, thanks for sharing the details. the fact that the task structure mapped cleanly to mint s job recording format is exactly the kind of signal i was hoping for. writing the work proof on-chain before settlement via recordjobcall makes a lot of sense, that s a solid primitive. i d definitely be interested in seeing the integration code if you re open to sharing it. even as a reference, it would help validate where the protocol boundaries should live (task envelope vs settlement vs execution). and i fully agree: the agent earns for work pattern feels underexplored, especially once you move beyond demos into real coordination and incentives. that s very much the direction i m thinking about. happy to compare notes as this evolves, this feels like a good overlap. cheers, joaquín reply to Name: 7_streaming_chunks_token_stream
Representative_Docs: ['this is a critical issue streaming that bypasses guardrails essentially negates the security layer entirely. the data is already exposed to the user before the middleware can block it. the fake-streaming workaround above works but sacrifices the ux benefit of streaming. a more robust pattern is to scan content in a buffered window during streaming accumulate n tokens, scan the buffer, flush if clean, block if flagged. clawmoat ( supports incremental buffered scanning which could work well here you can feed it chunks as they arrive and it maintains state across the stream, flagging as soon as a pattern is detected rather than waiting for the full response. this lets you preserve real streaming while still catching pii injection mid-stream. this is a broader architectural challenge though any middleware system that runs as a post-processing step will have this problem with streaming. the fix likely needs to happen at the langgraph middleware architecture level to support streaming-aware middleware.', "hi, i've done a thorough analysis of this issue and identified the root causes. i'd like to propose a fix and would be happy to submit a pr. root cause analysis bug 1: receives instead of when location: and the flow: 1. when and are provided, in correctly returns . 2. then falls through to call (the non-streaming path). 3. however, builds the payload via , which merges into the payload. 4. includes . when users instantiate with (or openai-compatible providers like dashscope vllm set it), the payload gets . 5. when is in the payload, returns an object, not a . 6. calling on this yields an , which then fails downstream when tries to access it as a . the sync has the same bug it shares identical logic. fix: in (and ), explicitly ensure in the payload before calling the api. these methods are specifically for non-streaming completion calls, so they should always use : and the same for sync . bug 2: disables streaming for all turns, not just tool-calling turns location: current code: the problem: the kwarg is typically passed via , which means it's present in every invocation of the model including turns that don't actually make tool calls (e.g., the model's final text response after a tool result is returned). the intent of is to disable streaming only when the model is expected to produce tool calls, but the current implementation disables it whenever is in the kwargs, which is always true for tool-bound models. this is actually by design per the docstring: will bypass streaming case only when the model is called with a keyword argument . however, users reasonably expect to mean disable streaming only on the specific turns where tool calls are generated, not on text-response turns . proposed approach: this second bug is more nuanced. i'd recommend keeping the current behavior for now and clarifying the documentation, since predicting whether the model will produce tool calls vs text at invocation time is not reliably possible. proposed changes 1. : force in both and payload, since these methods are only invoked on the non-streaming code path. 2. add regression tests: test with to verify the non-streaming fallback works correctly. 3. (optional) update docstring for to clarify the behavior scope. i'd love to take this on and submit a pr. could you assign this issue to me?", 'thanks this clarifies the request a lot. what you re seeing is expected today after switching to the correct input shape ( messages : ... ), getting only a complete message from agent.astream(...) astreamevents(...) is consistent with the current createagent implementation: - createagent s model node uses model.ainvoke() (not model.astream()), so token-level chunks are not propagated through the agent graph. - streammode messages in langgraph streams message events from nodes, but it can only be token-by-token if the node itself yields message chunks (e.g. aimessagechunk). if the node returns a single final aimessage, you ll see one event. so this is not a deep-thinking model breaks streaming bug. it s mainly: 1. input schema confusion (fixed now), and 2. expectation mismatch missing feature (token-wise streaming through createagent). doc wording if the docs say streams tuples of (token, metadata) from any graph nodes where an llm is invoked , it likely needs a clarification like: ...from nodes that stream (model.astream chunk-yielding nodes). if a node calls ainvoke and returns a final message, you will get a full message event instead of tokens. next steps - if your goal is token-wise streaming for sse: the reliable workaround is streaming directly from the model (model.astream) or using callbacks (onllmnewtoken) to push tokens to your sse layer. - if you specifically want createagent(...).astream(streammode messages ) to emit token chunks: this looks like a feature request (and is very similar to 34474 s observability nested agent streaming events request). i d suggest linking merging with 34474, and tracking it as an enhancement rather than a bug. if you can confirm whether you need (a) only token text chunks, or (b) a richer event stream (node tool start end, tool args results, etc.), it ll help define the scope.']

Name: 8_child_runname_config_middleware
Representative_Docs: ["feature eager tool dispatch middleware overlap tool execution with model streaming submission checklist - x this is a feature request, not a bug report or usage question. - x i added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes the feature request. - x i used the github search to find a similar feature request and didn't find it. - x i checked the langchain documentation and api reference to see if this feature already exists. - x this is not related to the langchain-community package. package - (specifically the middleware system) feature description proposal: a built-in middleware (or option on ) for eager tool dispatch firing each tool the moment its block finishes streaming, while the rest of the assistant response is still being generated. this is strictly faster than today's parallel tool calling (which waits for the full response before firing any tool) and produces identical model outputs. we've built and open-sourced a reference implementation as a langgraph middleware: ( it drops into the existing api with one line: same definitions, same prompt, same model outputs only the dispatch timing changes. use case today, already runs a single assistant message's tool calls in parallel that moves the tool phase from sum-of-durations to max-of-durations. but the stream still has to finish before the tool phase begins. for agents with multi-second tool calls (http fetches, db queries, rag, vector search, etc.), the model's stream and the tool execution are sequential, even though they could overlap. with eager dispatch, wall clock becomes instead of . numbers: - synthetic harness (16 workloads, 3 15 tools, deterministic): 1.20 1.50 over parallel dispatch, median 1.28 - cloudthinker production (real workloads with provider jitter): 50 median latency cut full benchmark: this pattern is most valuable for users running agents with sub-second-to-multi-second tools which describes most production agents today. proposed solution a middleware that: 1. adapts the model's streaming response (anthropic openai today; extensible) 2. detects per-block seal events (a new in the stream signals the previous block is complete) 3. dispatches the completed tool to an executor pool while the stream continues 4. surfaces results back to the agent loop without changing the existing contract our oss implementation already exposes the seal mechanism, a callable for non-idempotent tools (hitl), and for blanket safety. full mechanism doc: if the team is open to it, we'd be happy to: - contribute the middleware (or a stripped-down core) upstream - iterate on the contract to match the official middleware patterns - co-author docs examples alternatives considered - today's parallel tool calling: already provided by . eager dispatch is an additive improvement on top of it not a replacement. - manual streaming tool dispatch: users can implement this themselves by parsing the raw stream, but it requires forking the runner. a middleware keeps it transparent. - out-of-band tool prefetch via prompt hints: brittle, not generalizable. additional context - blog post with the full production story: - repo (mit): - when not to use it doc (sub-50ms tools, sequentially dependent tools, non-idempotent tools, non-streaming backends): happy to open a draft pr or rfc if the team is open to discussing an upstream path. thanks for everything you build!", "feature eager tool dispatch middleware overlap tool execution with model streaming submission checklist - x this is a feature request, not a bug report or usage Name: 9_deepseek_reasoning_reasoningcontent_thinking
Representative_Docs: ["langchain-deepseek: missing reasoningcontent passback in getrequestpayload causes 400 error on multi-turn agent calls description when using with deepseek models that support thinking mode (e.g., , ), calling fails on the second internal llm call with a 400 error. deepseek api requires that when an assistant message with is sent back to the api in a subsequent request, the field must be preserved in the message. however, does not pass through from the original to the api payload, causing the request to be rejected. error message full traceback reproduction code this code is for testing purposes only. root cause the issue is in . here's the data flow: 1. first llm call: deepseek returns an assistant message with 2. correctly extracts and stores it in 3. second llm call: calls to serialize the previous assistant message 4. does not merge into the top-level dict it only extracts standard openai fields ( , , ) 5. the resulting api payload is missing , causing deepseek api to return a 400 error fix the method needs to retrieve the original objects and pass through from to the api payload. patch applied to in : environment - : 1.0.1 - model: (also affects ) - os: windows 11 background i'm a college student from tianjin, china, currently learning the langchain framework. i encountered this issue while building a simple agent for testing purposes.", 'hi! i noticed the chatdeepseek has issues with reasoningcontent note here. i recently worked on 35094 (fix(deepseek): include reasoningcontent in assistant tool-call), which addresses deepseek thinking-mode tool-call replay: when an assistant message contains toolcalls, deepseek requires a top-level reasoningcontent field to be present in subsequent requests (empty-string fallback is acceptable). i added unit tests covering: - preserving reasoning_content when provided - injecting when missing to avoid 400s i d be happy to help drive the broader investigation. i work with deepseek models daily in production agent workflows and keep close track of deepseek s official api behavior docs and recent changes, so i can help distinguish expected upstream behavior vs. integration serialization issues. if useful, i can put together a small repro regression matrix across: - stream vs non-stream - multi-turn tool-call loops (assistant tool assistant) - interleaved reasoning - openrouter litellm vs direct deepseek api - strict beta endpoints', 'hi guys i have solve this issue by use langchain-deepseek but i found a new one deepseek thinking mode requires to be present on assistant messages that include toolcalls. langchain stores it in aimessage.additionalkwargs, but it was not serialized back into the request payload, causing 400 errors. description what happened when using langchain-deepseek chatdeepseek with deepseek thinking mode enabled (e.g. model deepseek-reasoner or extrabody thinking : type : enabled ) and the flow involves tool calling, the next request to deepseek fails with http 400. error: reasoningcontent why this happens official documentation) deepseek s docs require that in thinking mode, any assistant message that contains toolcalls must include a top-level reasoningcontent field when replayed in later requests (even if it s an empty string). otherwise the api returns 400. current langchain-deepseek behavior: 1 response parsing: if deepseek returns reasoningcontent, the integration stores it in aimessage.additionalkwargs reasoningcontent (and similarly for streaming chunks). 2 next request serialization: when building the next chat completions payload, langchain openai base serialization converts messages to dicts but does not serialize additionalkwargs into the outgoing payload. 3 as a result, the previous assistant message that includes toolcalls is sent back without the required top-level reasoningcontent, and deepseek rejects the request with 400. in short: reasoningcontent is captured in additionalkwargs, but it is not injected back into the outgoing assistant message dict, so thinking-mode tool-calls history fails deepseek validation. steps to reproduce 1 instantiate chatdeepseek with thinking mode enabled (deepseek-reasoner or extrabody.thinking.type enabled ). 2 bind at least one tool and trigger a tool call (assistant produces toolcalls). 3 when the agent graph sends the next request (after tool execution tool result message), deepseek returns http 400 complaining about missing reasoningcontent. expected behavior when thinking mode is enabled and an assistant message contains toolcalls, the outgoing payload should include a top-level reasoningcontent field for that assistant message (at minimum ) to satisfy deepseek s tool-calls thinking-mode requirements and avoid 400 errors. this is my issue code']

Name: 12_ai_deepseek_artifact_reasoningcontent
Representative_Docs: ['技术建议 这个问题涉及到 api 中 tool artifact 的传递 是一个很实际的 agent 监控 调试场景 根因分析 查看源码 事件目前只返回 工具返回内容 而 额外返回的数据 没有在事件payload中传递 这是因为事件系统设计上将 作为独立的数据流处理 解决方案建议 方案1 在事件中添加 artifact 字段 推荐 修改 中的事件发射逻辑 在 事件的 中添加 字段 这样用户就可以通过 访问工具的额外返回值 方案2 使用自定义事件 当前 workaround 的改进 用户已经发现的 workaround 是使用 但可以封装成更通用的模式 相关源码位置 - - 流式事件处理 - - 装饰器的 artifact 处理逻辑 测试用例建议 这个问题对于构建 agent 监控面板 调试工具非常重要 期待修复', '这个由于缺少 reasoningcontent 而导致的 400 错误 是提供商特定响应格式漂移导致多轮代理程序崩溃的典型案例 模型更改了其输出格式 而您的代码由于仅处理正常路径而出错 我们从另一个角度用 neuralbridge sdk 解决了这个问题 它不是试图预测每一种可能的响应格式 而是在 api 层诊断故障并自动恢复 无论这意味着使用调整后的参数重试 回退到不同的模型 还是完全切换提供商 就 deepseek 而言 我们已经多次遇到这种模式 自愈级联机制可以处理这种情况 无需手动解析格式 pip install neuralbridge-sdk 提供免费试用 联系方式 对 事实上通义那边也有类似的问题 只是表现形式不太一样 它不是像 deepseek 那样缺了 reasoningcontent 而是因为 chattongyi 这个客户端把请求端点写死了 一直用旧的 text-generation generation 结果像 qwen3.6-plus 这种新模型就直接给你甩一个 400 说 url error 我之前在内部排查的时候刚好也踩了这个坑 最后定位到 langchaincommunity.chatmodels.tongyi 的设计缺陷 它里面硬编码了 client dashscope.generation 永远只走旧端点 但实际上 dashscope 还有一个 openai 兼容的端点 compatible-mode v1 chat completions qwen3.6-plus 只在这个端点上才支持 更有意思的是 发布时间更晚的 deepseek-v4-flash 反而两个端点都能用 这就能看出来 阿里对自己家 新模型 和第三方模型在端点注册上的策略是不一样的 说到底 这也是典型的 api 行为漂移 模型可用的端点变了 但客户端 chattongyi 没有动态路由或者自愈能力 最后只能开发者自己手动换库或者改源码 所以我挺认同您前面提到的 neuralbridge sdk 那种思路 在 api 层做诊断 自动恢复 如果能把 模型 可用端点 这种探测和降级逻辑内置进去 比如先试旧端点 如果返回 400 或 401 就自动切到兼容端点 甚至直接跨提供商 fallback 那开发者就不用再纠结于 chattongyi 和 dashscope.completions 之间怎么选了', "这个问题本质上是因为qwen调用的是基类chatopenai的方法 chatopenai类中没有针对特定模型 包括qwen 的处理逻辑 而deepseek在 partners deepseek 下有独立的适配类 继承自chatopenai 这个类中有对于思考内容的输出处理逻辑 直接给qwen创建独立的适配类是不符合标准的 因为qwen的参数与返回结构严格遵守 openai 格式 除了可能多出思考字段 只要它是 接口兼容 就不应新建 partner 包或子类 所以我正在考虑别的方案 我愿意接手这个issue 请给我一周时间处理 the root of this problem is that qwen is calling the methods of the base class chatopenai, and the chatopenai class doesn't have specific processing logic for particular models (including qwen). in contrast, deepseek has an independent adapter class (inherited from chatopenai) under partners deepseek , which includes processing logic for the output of thinking content. creating an independent adapter class for qwen directly doesn't meet the standards, because qwen's parameters and return structure strictly adhere to the openai format (except for the potential addition of a thinking field). as long as it is interface compatible, a new partner package or subclass should not be created. therefore, i am considering other solutions. i am willing to take on the issue, please give me 1 week to handle."]

Name: 14_你好_reasoning_openai_sentence
Representative_Docs: ["i have ran some experiments by inferencing qwen using transformers from hugging face and ollama sdk which suggest that the issue is not in the langchain layer but rather in the ollama layer itself. the experiments below show that when is explicitly set, ollama still generates reasoning content instead of suppressing it. experiment 1: testing raw qwen model with think and nothink code ouput: observations from experiment 1: what was observed: - when using suffix: the model generates extensive reasoning inside tags before providing the answer - when using suffix: the model generates empty tags and provides a direct answer without reasoning reference: expected behavior: this is the correct behavior - the model properly respects the and directives when used directly with the raw transformers api. this demonstrates that the qwen model itself can properly control reasoning output. --- experiment 2: testing ollama chat api with think parameter testing ollama chat for reasoning code output observations from experiment 2: what was observed: 1. : - the model still generates reasoning thinking content - the reasoning is embedded in the field (not properly separated) - the field is 2. : - the thinking content is properly separated - the field contains the answer - the field contains the reasoning process - suggesting is default 3. : - same behavior as - the thinking content is properly separated into the field expected behavior: - : should produce a direct answer without any reasoning thinking content (similar to the behavior in experiment 1) - : current behavior is acceptable (default mode) - : current behavior is acceptable (explicitly enables thinking) the problem: when is explicitly set, ollama is not suppressing the reasoning content. instead, it's still generating the full thinking process but failing to separate it properly. this is a bug in the ollama layer, not in langchain. --- conclusion these experiments clearly demonstrate that: 1. the qwen model itself works correctly - when using the raw transformers api with and suffixes, the model properly generates or suppresses reasoning content as expected. 2. the ollama layer has a bug with the parameter - when is set through ollama's chat api, the reasoning content is not suppressed. instead, the model continues to generate extensive thinking reasoning, but it gets embedded in the field rather than being separated into the field. 3. this is not a langchain issue - the problem occurs at the ollama api level, as demonstrated by calling ollama's chat function directly without any langchain wrapper. the correct behavior for should be to suppress reasoning entirely and produce only a direct answer, similar to how the suffix works with the raw model. currently, ollama is not respecting this parameter correctly.", "investigation summary after thorough investigation of this issue, i've determined that this is expected behavior rather than a bug, though it highlights a need for better documentation around the responses api configuration. understanding the issue when using with and reasoning enabled, reasoning blocks from the model's response are included in subsequent api requests. this causes errors in two scenarios: 1. non-openai endpoints (like doubao in this case) that don't fully implement openai's responses api spec 2. missing configuration that leads to format mismatches why this behavior is correct for openai according to openai's documentation on handling function calls with reasoning models ( it is essential that we preserve any reasoning and function call responses in our conversation history. this is how the model keeps track of what chain-of-thought steps it has run through. the api will error if these are not included. and from the responses api reasoning items guide ( if a turn includes a function call (which may require an extra round trip outside the api), you do need to include the reasoning items either via or by explicitly adding the reasoning item to . so langchain is correctly including reasoning items as required by openai's api. the real issue the problem occurs because: 1. for non-openai endpoints: these apis may not support reasoning blocks in input at all, making the responses api incompatible 2. missing configuration: without explicit , format inconsistencies can occur solution the workaround mentioned by is correct: why this works: parameter effect ----------- -------- ensures aimessage content uses the correct v1 format for the responses api instead of sending reasoning blocks back, uses the response id to let the server maintain reasoning state. this avoids sending reasoning blocks in input entirely. for non-openai endpoints if your endpoint doesn't support : 1. disable reasoning mode: 2. use chat completions api instead: don't set recommendation i suggest closing this issue as works as designed since: 1. the current behavior follows openai's specification 2. working solutions exist via proper configuration 3. non-openai endpoints may have varying levels of responses api support however, it would be valuable to improve documentation around: - required configuration when using reasoning with - compatibility considerations for non-openai endpoints - the relationship between , , and reasoning --- sources: - openai cookbook: handling function calls with reasoning models ( - openai cookbook: better performance from reasoning models using the responses api ( - openai blog: why we built the responses api (", "i think i have identified the problem. the issue lies in qwen3.5:9b on ollama. by default, it outputs the thinking process (thinking) and the final answer (content). however, ollamallm, as the api generate interface, uses all the tokens for the thinking process. if i directly use the command line, i can see: c: users 86189 ollama run qwen3.5:9b 你好 请用一句话回答 thinking... thinking process: 1. analyze the request: user: 你好 请用一句话回答 (hello, please answer in one sentence.) constraint: 一句话 (one sentence). language: chinese. 2. determine the content: the user is greeting me and setting a constraint. i should acknowledge the greeting and the constraint. i need to keep it concise and within a single sentence. 3. drafting options: option 1: 你好 我会按照你的要求用一句话回答你 (hello! i will answer you according to your request in one sentence.) - a bit meta. option 2: 你好 很高兴为你服务 (hello, happy to serve you.) - good, but maybe doesn't explicitly acknowledge the constraint. option 3: 你好 请说吧 我准备好了 (hello, go ahead, i'm ready.) - okay. option 4: 你好 没问题 (hello, no problem.) - short and sweet. option 5: 你好 我会尽力用一句话来回复你 (hello, i will try my best to reply in one sentence.) - explicitly acknowledges the constraint. 4. selecting the best option: since the instruction is answer in one sentence , the most direct and compliant response is to greet back and confirm compliance. 你好 没问题 我会用一句话回答你 (hello, no problem, i will answer you in one sentence.) - a bit redundant if i'm already answering in one sentence. 你好 请说你的问题 我会用一句话回答 (hello, ask your question, i will answer in one sentence.) simple greeting compliance: 你好 我明白了 我会用一句话来回复你 even simpler: 你好 很高兴为你服务 请提问 but the user specifically asked to answer in one sentence . so the response itself must be one sentence. let's make it natural. 你好 没问题 请说你想聊的内容 (hello, no problem, please tell me what you want to talk about.) or simply: 你好 没问题 请讲 (hello, no problem, go ahead.) actually, acknowledging the constraint is often better for instruction following. 你好 我会严格遵守你的要 求只用一句话回答 let's go with a friendly, compliant response. 你好 没问题 我会用一句话来回复你 (hello, no problem, i will reply to you in one sentence.) - wait, if i say i will reply in one sentence and then do it, that's a bit wordy. how about: 你好 请说你想问的问题 我会用一句话回答 or just: 你好 请继续提问 我会用一句话回复 let's try to be polite and concise. 你好 很高兴为你服务 请随时提问 (hello, happy to serve, please ask anytime.) wait, the prompt is hello, please answer in one sentence . the response is the answer. so, 你好 没问题 我会用一句话回复你 counts as one sentence. better: 你好 请说你想了解的内容 我会用一句话回复 simplest: 你好 没问题 请提问 let's pick a balanced one: 你好 没问题 我会严格遵守要求用一句话回答 (hello, no problem, i will strictly follow the requirement and answer in one sentence.) - still one sentence. or just: 你好 请说吧 我会用一句话回答 actually, the most natural response to hello, answer in one sentence is hello, no problem. followed by something else to make it one sentence. 你好 没问题 请提出你的问题吧 let's refine: 你好 我明白了 会用一句话来回复你 (hello, i understand, i will use one sentence to reply to you.) or: 你好 请提问 我会用一句话回答 let's go with: 你好 没问题 我会用一句话来回复你 wait, i can just say: 你好 请说 我会用一句话回答 but maybe just acknowledging the greeting is enough? no, need to acknowledge the constraint implicitly or explicitly. let's try: 你好 没问题 请随时提出你的问题 okay, final decision: 你好 没问题 我会用一句话来回复你 wait, i should check if hello, i will answer in one sentence counts as one sentence. yes. let's make it more conversational. 你好 请讲 我会用一句话回答 okay, i'll choose: 你好 没问题 我会用一句话来回答你 wait, looking at the instruction again: 请用一句话回答 (please answer using one sentence). if i say i will answer in one sentence , that's future tense. maybe just: 你好 请提问 我会严格遵守只用一句话的要求 let's keep it simple. 你好 请提问 我会用一句话回答 actually, the best response that shows intelligence and compliance is: 你好 没问题 我会用一句话来回复你 wait, is there a way to make it even shorter? 你好 请说 我准备好了 let's go with: 你好 没问题 请提问 (hello, no problem, please ask.) - this implies compliance without explicitly stating the constraint in the text, but the text itself is one sentence. however, explicitly acknowledging the constraint is safer for instruction following evaluation. 你好 我会遵守你的要求 用一句话回答你 let's settle on: 你好 没问题 我会用一句话来回复你 wait, i can just say: 你好 请说吧 我会用一句话回答 let's make it slightly more natural. 你好 没问题 请提问吧 no, acknowledge the constraint. 你好 我会用一句话来回复你 this is one sentence. it acknowledges the constraint and greets. let's try to be a bit more engaging. 你好 很高兴为你服务 Name: 15_state_turn_graph_fix
Representative_Docs: ["thank you for your updated test using ! i've run comprehensive tests and can confirm the bug is still present. test results summary test method result ------ -------- -------- direct agent.invoke() - single call pass direct agent.invoke() - two step with checkpointer pass wrapped graph - no checkpointer fail wrapped graph - with checkpointer fail wrapped graph - two step with checkpointer fail both with checkpointers agent graph checkpointers fail reducer in isolation function pass pattern: all direct calls pass, all calls fail. root cause identified the bug is specifically about values being lost when state is merged back from a subgraph to the parent graph. i tested with both a value (to delete) and a regular value (to add) in the same call: results: direct : wrapped : key observation from debug output: the reducer correctly deletes (we see the log), but when the state is merged back to the parent graph, the deletion is lost. the value is somehow being filtered out or the original state is being restored. environment tested the issue may be in how langgraph merges state from subgraphs back to parent graphs. specifically: - regular value updates via work correctly in nested graphs - values (used to delete keys via custom reducers) are lost ignored when state is returned to the parent graph this affects any production workflow that: 1. uses with a custom 2. wraps the agent in a (common multi-node workflow pattern) 3. uses with values to delete state keys tell me if i am doing something wrong, and thank you again for your help!", "thank you for the working code. i have tried to incrementally add to your test my production pattern. the suggested fix for handling values in (passing to ) works in simple cases but fails when the agent is wrapped in a stategraph, which is a common production pattern. progressive reproduction results we tested the official fix with incremental complexity: test configuration result ------ --------------- -------- test 1 direct with works test 2 middleware works test 3 wrapped in stategraph (start agent end) fails test 4 pre post processing nodes fails test 5 checkpointer (persistence) fails breaking point: test 3 - stategraph wrapping the fix works when calling directly, but breaks when the same agent is added as a node in a stategraph. minimal reproduction see for complete working code. here's the key difference: works - direct invoke (test 1) fails - wrapped in stategraph (test 3) root cause analysis when is used directly: 1. tool returns 2. agent's internal state handling respects the and calls the reducer 3. reducer correctly deletes the item via 4. deletion works when the same agent is wrapped in a stategraph: 1. tool returns 2. agent completes and returns its state update to the graph 3. somewhere in the graph's state merging, the value is filtered out 4. reducer never receives the value 5. item persists hypothesis: the stategraph's state merging logic filters values before calling reducers, even when is properly configured. state configuration (all correct) our state follows all the recommendations: impact this affects any production usage where: - is used (recommended approach) - agent is wrapped in a stategraph (common pattern for multi-step workflows) - tools need to delete items via in example real-world scenarios: - multi-agent systems with agent agent routing - workflows with pre-processing agent post-processing - any graph with checkpointer for persistence environment - langchain-core: 1.2.6 - langchain: 1.1.9 - langgraph: 0.2.66 - python: 3.11 - os: macos request the official fix ( parameter) is incomplete. it works for direct agent invocation but not when the agent is used in a stategraph, which is a core langgraph pattern. please investigate: 1. why does work for direct but not when wrapped in ? 2. is there additional configuration needed for stategraph scenarios? 3. should stategraph's state merging preserve values when reducers are configured to handle them? reproduction script run to see all 5 progressive tests and exact breaking point. key files: - - progressive tests showing where it breaks - this report previous workaround we previously implemented a workaround using marker patterns in tool messages and a separate graph node to re-apply deletions. this shouldn't be necessary if the official fix worked in stategraph scenarios. related issues - original issue about filtering values - this update demonstrates the fix is incomplete for stategraph usage minimalreproduction_steps.py (", "qeek.ai agent : the analysis in the issue is correct. here's a full technical breakdown of the root cause and the proposed fix . root cause the bug is caused by the combination of langgraph's state persistence and the conditional routing logic in . 1. state persistence when a new turn starts on the same thread, is loaded from the checkpoint. if the previous turn ended with a , that value is present in state at the start of the next turn. 2. missing clear path in (lines 196 225), is only added to the state update if it is not : if the model's first attempt in the new turn fails validation, is . the update dict does not contain the key, leaving the stale value from the previous turn intact in the graph state. 3. premature exit the routing edges and check for the presence of the key in state to decide whether to exit: since the stale value is still there, the agent exits immediately and returns the answer from the previous turn. proposed fix two targeted changes in : change 1 always update in ensure every model execution explicitly sets the field, overwriting any stale values with when no new response has been successfully parsed. change 2 update routing edges to check for both (line 1752) and (line 1779) should check for a non-none value, not just key presence. alternative considered: making an (similar to ) was considered. while this would automatically clear the field between turns, it would prevent from being persisted in the checkpoint for auditing or external state inspection a potentially breaking change for existing users. the fix above is safer: it maintains the current persistence model while ensuring freshness during execution. summary - root cause: stale checkpoint value key-presence routing check premature exit - fix scope: 3 lines changed across 2 functions in - backwards compatible: checkpoint persistence behavior is unchanged - no new dependencies or interface changes required"]

Name: 16_decision_reasoning_time_state
Representative_Docs: ["the pep 578 hook approach has a subtle problem beyond the architecture issue: audit hooks fire at the python interpreter level, which means you're capturing execution trace, not decision context. by the time the hook fires, the reasoning state that produced the action is already gone. what you actually want to freeze is the input context, tool selection rationale, and model state at decision time, before execution. replay then becomes deterministic comparison against that snapshot, not a re-run of bytecode. did the v1.1 architecture address capturing pre-execution reasoning state, or did it stay focused on the execution layer?", "governance adapters at the framework level are useful, but one gap i've seen in production: they capture what the agent did, not why it decided that at the moment of execution. the reasoning context, tool selection rationale, and prompt state at decision time often differ from what you can reconstruct after the fact from logs. especially when model versions or chain configs drift between runs. are you capturing a frozen snapshot of the reasoning inputs at decision time, or reconstructing from execution traces?", "governance adapters at the framework level are useful, but one gap i keep hitting in production: the adapter captures what the agent did, not the reasoning state at decision time. by the time a compliance audit runs, the context window, tool outputs, and intermediate chain state are gone. logging the action is necessary but often not sufficient for 'why did it do that' questions. are you capturing a frozen snapshot of the full reasoning context at decision time, or primarily the inputs outputs at each step?"]

Name: 17_tool_loop_limits_recursion
Representative_Docs: ['security: recursive tool calling without cycle guard security findings report we scanned this repository using inkog ( an ai security scanner, and identified 1 medium severity vulnerability related to recursive tool calling. summary - 1 medium severity recursive tool calling without cycle guard - governance score: 100 100 (otherwise excellent security posture) findings severity issue location ---------- ------- ---------- medium recursive tool calling without cycle guard details the code allows recursive tool calling without implementing a cycle detection or depth limit guard. while this might be intentional for certain use cases, it poses risks: - stack overflow: deep recursion can exhaust memory - infinite loops: malformed or adversarial inputs could cause the agent to recurse indefinitely - resource exhaustion: can lead to dos conditions how to reproduce you can verify these findings by running inkog yourself: recommendations 1. add recursion depth limit: implement a maximum recursion depth counter. 2. cycle detection: track call history to detect and prevent circular tool calling patterns. 3. timeout mechanisms: add configurable timeout limits for tool execution chains. 4. documentation: if recursive behavior is intentional, document the risks and provide configuration options for users to set their own limits. learn more for detailed remediation guidance and best practices for securing ai applications, visit inkog.io ( --- this report was generated to help improve the security of your project. we hope you find it useful! note: your repository scored 100 100 on governance, which is excellent this is just a minor improvement opportunity.', 'this is a thoughtful proposal! the proactive approach makes sense informing the llm about tool call limits upfront can lead to more intentional tool usage rather than reactive panic when limits are hit. here are a few considerations for the implementation: architecture suggestion the hook approach is solid. you could extend the existing with a new config flag: implementation strategy 1. track remaining calls: maintain a counter accessible via 2. inject contextual message: use hook to insert a with remaining count 3. graceful degradation: if , fall back to current reactive behavior potential enhancements - adaptive warnings: instead of every turn, warn when - tool-family specific limits: different limits per tool category - token budget integration: combine with total token limits for better planning existing patterns to follow check for how tool execution is tracked, and for the callback pattern. happy to review the pr when you are ready!', "this is a thoughtful proposal! the proactive approach makes sense informing the llm about tool call limits upfront can lead to more intentional tool usage rather than reactive panic when limits are hit. here are a few considerations for the implementation: architecture suggestion the hook approach is solid. you could extend the existing with a new config flag: implementation strategy 1. track remaining calls: maintain a counter accessible via 2. inject contextual message: use to insert a with remaining count 3. graceful degradation: if , fall back to current reactive behavior potential enhancements - adaptive warnings: instead of every turn, warn when - tool-family specific limits: different limits per tool category - token budget integration: combine with total token limits for better planning existing patterns to follow check for how tool execution is tracked, and for the callback pattern. happy to review the pr when you're ready!"]

Name: 18_prompt_context_message_smaller
Representative_Docs: ["hi, thanks for the interest everyone this is a useful improvement. before assigning, it d be great if one of you could propose a concrete prompt template (with example inputs outputs and model comparisons, especially for smaller hf models). that will help us evaluate and avoid overlap. happy to review once that s shared on sun, mar 29, 2026, 4:56 am gloris cedeno . wrote: glorisdenisse left a comment (langchain-ai langchain 36226) hello imen-source gloris i've actually worked on a similar problem in a recent paper i just published at acm chiir 2026 (collabsearch, doi: 10.1145 3786304.3788848 role-playing prompts (arpp) for a smaller open model (llama 3.0-8b) and found that role anchoring explicit context scoping structured fallback paths significantly reduced vague evasive responses by improving the prompt structure. i think this can help here and matches your proposed solution. if it doesn't work properly, i also read another relevant paper that had some strategies for prompt enhancement. i'd like to contribute based on this approach could this be assigned to me? reply to this email directly, view it on github or unsubscribe . you are receiving this because you are subscribed to this thread.message id: .", "unified structured output and tool-calling interface in basechatmodel checked other resources - x this is a feature request, not a bug report or usage question. - x i added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes the feature request. - x i used the github search to find a similar feature request and didn't find it. - x i checked the langchain documentation and api reference to see if this feature already exists. - x this is not related to the langchain-community package. feature description introduce a first-class, provider-agnostic api in basechatmodel for: declaring structured outputs via json schema . a unified responseformat interface that normalizes openai json mode , azure, anthropic, google, etc. built-in validation, automatic retry with model-aware prompts, and partial streamed structured outputs. standardized tool-calling with typed schemas, deterministic tool-selection hints, and safe batching across turns. this aims to reduce provider-specific branching, minimize prompt gymnastics, and improve reliability for structured outputs and tool calls across chatmodels. use case developers need consistent json-structured outputs across providers without bespoke prompts per vendor. agents using tools require deterministic tool selection and robust schema validation retries to avoid malformed payloads. pipelines that depend on partial streaming structured data (e.g., early fields for ui, late fields for analytics) need a common abstraction. enterprise users need auditable, typed outputs with strict validation and fallback behaviors to meet reliability sla requirements. proposed solution add a responseformat option to basechatmodel apis that accepts: schema: json schema (draft 2020-12) or pydantic model (auto-converted). strict: boolean or strategy enum (e.g., strict, tolerant, coercive). onvalidationerror: policy (retry, repair, raise, returnraw). maxretries, repairprompttemplate, and coercionrules. provide a unified json mode adapter internally that maps to provider-native capabilities (e.g., openai responseformat, anthropic tool json hints, google structured outputs) or emulates via prompting when absent. expose streaming structured outputs: token-level stream with incremental json assembly. field-level stream for known keys (emit when valid). standardize tool-calling in basechatmodel: tools: list of typed tool specs (name, description, json schema for args). toolchoice: enum (auto, required, none, or specific tool). built-in validation and auto-retry for malformed tool args, with optional schema coercion. support tool-call batching and carry-over across turns with stable tool ids. add callback hooks telemetry: pre post validation events, retry events, tool selection and arg validation events, and structured streaming events. alternatives considered no response additional context no response_", "hi! i ran tests to reproduce the findings and investigate further. here's what i found: test setup: - pdf: layout-parser-paper.pdf (research paper, 16 pages) - models: google flan-t5-small and google flan-t5-large - evaluated prompt templates against 6 factual questions with known correct answers from the document on the prompt problem: you're right that the current langchain default prompt causes issues with smaller models. the line if you don't know the answer, just say that you don't know is the root cause smaller models follow that instruction even when the answer is in the context. i tested the following prompt vs the proposed one vs the current one: act as a research assistant. using only the document context below, answer the question. context: context question: question answer directly and concisely using only information from the context. if the information is not found, say information not found . for the smaller model imen's propostion had the best results. however, fixing the prompt alone is not enough for the flan-t5-large model. on flan-t5-large it becomes too conservative, returning not found in document for 3 out of 6 questions, even when the answers were present in the document. results (flan-t5-large): prompt vague abstained correct --- --- --- langchain default 0 6 1 6 proposed prompt 3 6 1 6 the real bottleneck is not just the prompt wording it's that retrieved chunks contain too much noise for small models to extract the right answer from. i propose adding an optional context summarization step that pre-filters the retrieved chunks down to question-relevant facts before passing them to the prompt. this significantly improves results: prompt vague abstained correct --- --- --- proposed prompt context summary 2 6 1 6 research assistant prompt context summary 1 6 2 6 question: - you mentioned getting better results with another pdf could you share it, or test on the same document so we can compare fairly? right now i'm implementing context summarization as an optional flag that auto-enables for larger local models, since i tried with the smaller model and it didn't improve the answer at all, since it's too small to handle a 2-step pipeline. for the smaller ones, i recommend the already proposed prompt."]

Representative_Docs: ["qeek.ai agent: the analysis in the issue is correct. here's a full technical breakdown of the root cause and the proposed fix . root cause the with status code is most likely caused by sending values for optional parameters in the json request body. in , several optional fields are unconditionally included in the params dict even when their value is : these serialize as in the json payload sent to the ollama server. while the ollama api itself may handle gracefully in some versions, two things make this fragile: 1. proxies and gateways the id in the error message ( ) is a strong indicator the request is passing through a proxy or ingress controller. many proxies perform strict schema validation and reject values for fields they expect to be typed (booleans, strings, etc.). 2. newly introduced fields the parameter was added recently for reasoning support. older ollama server versions or provider-hosted instances that haven't picked up this field will likely reject for an unknown key. the library surfaces this as a with status code , which it uses when the error originates from the response body rather than the http status line consistent with a proxy injecting an error into an otherwise 200-envelope response. proposed fix update to only include optional parameters when they are explicitly set, matching how is already handled elsewhere in the same method: this ensures the json payload sent to the server only contains fields the user has explicitly configured, making it compatible with strict proxies, older server versions, and hosted ollama instances that don't yet support newer parameters. test coverage a unit test should verify that omits -valued fields from the output: note on the fuzzer environment the context is worth calling out separately. a high-volume fuzzer hitting a proxy-fronted ollama instance will surface rate limiting, timeout injection, and schema validation failures that wouldn't appear in normal usage. even after this fix, users running fuzzers against proxy-hosted endpoints should expect some occurrences that originate at the proxy layer rather than the langchain integration. impact assessment - scope: conditional guards added in one method in - risk: low omitting fields is strictly less likely to cause errors than including them; any server that requires these fields to be present would need to document that explicitly - backwards compatibility: no interface change; behavior is identical when fields are explicitly set summary the root cause is unconditional inclusion of -valued optional parameters in the json payload, which strict proxies and older ollama versions reject. the fix adds simple guards consistent with how the rest of the method already handles optional values.", "thanks for the report. we Name: 21_argument_dict_type_indentation
Representative_Docs: ['core: defaulttoolparser crashes on malformed function dicts, silently drops all valid tool calls checked other resources - x i searched existing issues and this has not been reported before example code error message and stack trace description and in crash with unhandled when a raw tool call has a malformed field. root cause: both functions check but do not validate that the value is actually a dict with the expected keys. they use direct indexing ( ) instead of . impact: when any single tool call in a list is malformed, the exception kills the entire parsing loop. the caller in catches broadly, so all tool calls (including valid ones) are silently dropped. this is problematic because: 1. the function is documented as best-effort parsing but is actually all-or-nothing 2. already has an return list for exactly this purpose, but malformed function dicts never reach it affected functions: - (line 361): - (lines 405-406): and system info langchain-core latest (main branch as of 2025-07-12)', "core: parsegoogledocstring mishandles continuation lines containing colons bug description in incorrectly parses multi-line argument descriptions when a continuation line contains a colon. the continuation line is treated as a new argument definition instead of being appended to the current argument's description. reproduction expected: 2 args: (description: the search query to use for finding things: important ones ), actual: 3 args: , (treated as a new arg name), root cause the parser uses to detect new argument lines without considering indentation. in google-style docstrings, continuation lines have deeper indentation than argument definition lines, but the parser doesn't distinguish between them. proposed fix detect the base indentation level from the first argument line and treat any line with deeper indentation as a continuation of the current argument's description, regardless of whether it contains a colon.", "hi! i'd like to work on this issue. i've reviewed the bug and the root cause is clear the parser uses to detect new argument lines without considering indentation, so continuation lines containing colons are incorrectly treated as new argument definitions. my proposed approach: detect the base indentation level from the first argument line in the section, and treat any line with deeper indentation as a continuation of the current argument's description, regardless of whether it contains a colon. i have a fix ready and will submit a pr once assigned. could i be assigned to this issue? thanks!"]

Name: 26_mcp_discovery_agent_tool
Representative_Docs: ["feature request add mcp discovery tool for dynamic mcp server discovery feature request add a langchain tool wrapper for mcp discovery api, enabling agents to dynamically discover mcp servers. motivation langchain agents are limited to pre-configured tools. with mcp discovery, agents could: - discover new mcp servers based on task requirements - get performance metrics before choosing a tool - adapt their toolset dynamically proposed solution what mcp discovery provides - semantic search - 24 mcp servers indexed - performance metrics - latency, uptime data - server comparison - side-by-side analysis - free tier - 100 queries month links - github: - npm: - api: happy to submit a pr if there's interest!", "native mcp code execution support would be a significant addition. the lazy result loading aspect is particularly important for agent use cases. from our experience running 31 agents that execute code via mcp: why lazy result loading matters for agents: when an agent calls an mcp code execution tool that returns a large result (e.g., a dataframe with 10k rows), loading the full result into the agent's context window is wasteful. the agent typically only needs a summary or the first n rows to make decisions. lazy loading lets the agent request just what it needs. our approach: 1. tool returns a result handle (reference id metadata: row count, column names, data types) 2. agent inspects the metadata and decides what to load (first 10 rows, specific columns, aggregations) 3. only the requested slice is loaded into context this reduces token consumption by 80-90 for data-heavy tool calls. on programmatic tool calling: the key insight is that mcp tools should be composable. an agent should be able to chain multiple mcp tool calls in a single reasoning step without round-tripping through the llm for each call. we implement this as a tool call plan the agent outputs a sequence of tool calls, the runtime executes them in order (or in parallel where possible), and only the final result is fed back to the llm. security consideration: code execution tools need the strongest governance. we classify them as destructive tier requiring capability verification and budget check before execution. the agent's sandbox is isolated (network namespace, filesystem isolation, resource limits) to prevent tool-call-as-exploit. architecture patterns for tool execution and governance: context management for token-efficient tool results:", "strong feature request the intersection of mcp and programmatic tool calling is where agent frameworks need to go next. from integrating mcp tools with openclaw agents in production, a few patterns that might inform the langchain implementation: on lazy result loading: this is essential for mcp tools that return large payloads (code execution output, database query results, file contents). we implement a two-phase pattern: the tool returns a lightweight result descriptor (type, size, summary hash), and the agent decides whether to fetch the full payload. this prevents a single tool call from blowing the context window the agent can choose to work with the summary or explicitly request the full result. on code execution specifically: the biggest gap in current mcp code execution is sandboxing with state persistence. agents need to execute code across multiple turns while maintaining the execution environment (variables, imports, file system state). we use per-agent isolated runtimes with session affinity the same agent always hits the same execution sandbox, and state persists until the session ends or the agent explicitly resets. on the interaction with tool use patterns: mcp tools should integrate with the agent's existing tool retry and error handling. when a code execution tool fails, the agent needs structured error information (not just stderr), including: which line failed, what the variable state was at failure, and suggested fixes. this is a different error contract than typical mcp tools and should be part of the specification. per-agent tool configuration: in multi-agent setups, different agents need different mcp tool permissions and configurations. we use per-agent plugin config overrides a research agent gets read-only filesystem access, a coding agent gets read-write, and a review agent gets execution-only. this granularity should be part of how langchain surfaces mcp tools to agents. architecture reference on multi-tenant tool configuration: multi-agent coordination patterns (relevant for shared mcp resources):"]

Representative_Docs: ["add support for cockroachdb vector data and indexing checked other resources - x this is a feature request, not a bug report or usage question. - x i added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes the feature request. - x i used the github search to find a similar feature request and didn't find it. - x i checked the langchain documentation and api reference to see if this feature already exists. - x this is not related to the langchain-community package. feature description i would like langchain to support adding cockroachdb vector data and indexing as a supported integration for vector databases. see use case it would allow for a scalable and durable vector data store. proposed solution no response alternatives considered no response additional context no response", "feature request: add native opensearch vector database support for vector storage and retrieval checked other resources - x this is a feature request, not a bug report or usage question. - x i added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes the feature request. - x i used the github search to find a similar feature request and didn't find it. - x i checked the langchain documentation and api reference to see if this feature already exists. - x this is not related to the langchain-community package. feature description summary langchain currently supports various vector databases like pinecone, chroma, faiss, and weaviate, but lacks native support for opensearch (formerly elasticsearch oss). opensearch is a widely-used, open-source search and analytics engine with robust vector search capabilities that would benefit the langchain community. motivation enterprise adoption: many organizations already use opensearch for logging, monitoring, and search, making it a natural choice for vector storage cost-effective: open-source alternative to proprietary vector databases scalability: proven ability to handle large-scale deployments feature rich: supports hybrid search (combining vector and keyword search), filtering, and advanced analytics self-hosted: allows organizations to maintain data sovereignty and control current workaround users currently need to implement custom vector store classes or use generic elasticsearch integrations, which don t fully leverage opensearch s vector capabilities. use case key features to support basic vector operations: document ingestion with automatic embedding vector similarity search (cosine, euclidean, dot product) metadata filtering batch operations advanced features: hybrid search (vector keyword) maximum marginal relevance (mmr) search custom scoring functions index management and optimization opensearch specific: support for different vector engines (nmslib, faiss, lucene) index templates and mappings cluster management security features (authentication, encryption) proposed solution no response alternatives considered no response additional context no response", "valkey vector store support checked other resources - x this is a feature request, not a bug report or usage question. - x i added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes the feature request. - x i used the github search to find a similar feature request and didn't find it. - x i checked the langchain documentation and api reference to see if this feature already exists. - x this is not related to the langchain-community package. package (required) - x langchain-aws feature description valkey is an open-source fork of redis suitable for high-speed in-memory kv caching with its own native vector database and search engine (hnsw or flat), batching, pipelining, and cluster capabilities. it is used by a significant share of the market warranting solid support for this backend. while the current redis vector store adapter can theoretically be used with a valkey backend, it is not actually possible as there are protocol incompatibilities. therefore, langchain presently cannot be plugged into a valkey backend. apropos, we propose a custom valkey vector store adapter. use case this would provide a langchain vector store adapter for users with a valkey back-end. this would include aws elasticache users. there is an existing redis adapter that should accomplish the same goal, however, a valkey-specific vector store adapter ensures valkey back-end compatibility. proposed solution implement langchain's abstract class for valkey functionality integrating it with the valkey glide api. in addition to meeting the requirements of the contract, the adapter will also support, implicitly: - pipelining - batching - cluster connectivity alternatives considered one proposed alternative was to use the existing redis adapter for valkey backends, but there are some protocol differences that presently make this unsuitable, hence warranting the need for a valkey-specific vector store adapter. additional context the valkey vector store adapter is an alternative to the existing redis vector store adapter for langchain. ideally one would (could) use the redis adapter with a valkey backend, however: - redis adapter cannot presently be used with valkey-search backend due to this open issue: implement sortby option for ft.search command ( therefore it's worth implementing a valkey-specific vector store adapter to work around the protocol differences with redis."]

Name: 30_loop_cache_pr_performance
Representative_Docs: ["hi, i've updated my pr with a revised fix that addresses both the original cross-loop bug and the performance regression from my first attempt. root cause recap: returns a process-global cached . stores it at time. when a second uses the same instance, requests go through a client bound to the now-closed first loop. new approach loop-aware proxy: instead of a plain 'd client, the function now returns a itself 'd (one per pair, so init performance is preserved). the proxy overrides to dispatch to a per-loop inner stored in a . each new event loop gets a fresh inner client; gc'd loops are cleaned up automatically. this avoids the -98.55 regression because the outer proxy is created once and cached pays no per-loop overhead. the per-loop cost is only on the first from a new loop. could a maintainer please assign this issue to me so the pr can be reopened? thank you!", "i'm trying to reproduce this on my side on mon, mar 16, 2026, 3:57 pm saiiiii . wrote: saiprasanth-git left a comment (langchain-ai langchain 35783) hi maintainers! i opened pr 35794 with a weakvaluedictionary per-event-loop cache, but it was auto-closed since i wasn't assigned. i also noticed the codspeed performance regression (-98.55 on testinittime) flagged on my pr. the root cause is that weakvaluedictionary.getitem is significantly slower than lrucache for the init-time benchmark. the fix shivam described (a loop-aware proxy wrapper that stays at the outer level) is a cleaner approach and would preserve init performance. could a maintainer please assign this issue to me so i can update my pr ( 35794 proxy-based approach? happy to implement it right away. reply to this email directly, view it on github or unsubscribe . you are receiving this because you are subscribed to this thread.message id: .", "hi maintainers! i opened pr 35794 fixing this issue with a per-event-loop cache, but it was auto-closed since i wasn't assigned. i also noticed the codspeed performance regression (-98.55 on ) flagged on my pr. the root cause is that is significantly slower than for the init-time benchmark. the fix shivam described (a loop-aware proxy wrapper that stays d at the outer level) is a cleaner approach and would preserve init performance. could a maintainer please assign this issue to me so i can update my pr ( 35794) with the proxy-based approach? happy to implement it right away."]

Name: 33_llm_prompt_connection pooling_pooling
Representative_Docs: ['for now, i have disabled the streaming. also, make sure to check the tokens consumptions, for me the input token increased because they were charging for each llm inference call. for example - if they do tool search tool use the new tools to generate the output, you will be charged 2x because they need to process the prompt twice.', 'this httpx client recreation is a real performance killer at scale. quick fix - use a singleton client: or connection pooling: the overhead of creating new tls connections for each request can add 100-300ms latency. --- building llm pipelines? we are hiring at revolutionai:', "this is a common pattern issue with llm wrappers. until it's fixed upstream, here are some workarounds: workaround 1: singleton pattern workaround 2: connection pooling at the httpx level workaround 3: if you're using this in a web server initialize the llm client at startup, not per-request: the root issue is that the sdk should be reusing clients internally. would be great to see this fixed at the library level!"]
